stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dbinom{2n}{n}\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{n} \). Apply the ratio test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| \).
solution: Since \( \binom{2n}{n} = \dfrac{(2n)!}{(n!)^2} \), the ratio is \( \dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n} = \dfrac{(2n+2)(2n+1)}{(n+1)^2}\cdot\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \). As \( n\to\infty \) the fraction tends to \( 4 \), so \( L = 4\cdot\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the geometric ratio \( \frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), forgetting that \( \binom{2n}{n} \) contributes a factor of \( 4 \).
This inverts the geometric ratio (and keeps the \( 4 \)).
This uses a factor of \( 2 \) instead of \( 4 \) for the binomial growth.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dfrac{1}{n!}\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{n} \). Apply the ratio test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| \).
solution: \( \dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n} = \dfrac{1}{n+1}\cdot\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), and as \( n\to\infty \) the factor \( \dfrac{1}{n+1}\to 0 \), so \( L = 0 \). Since \( L=0 < 1 \), the factorial in the denominator forces convergence regardless of \( p,q \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \( \frac{1}{n+1}\to 0 \) factor entirely, treating it as if it were a plain geometric series.
This drops the \( \frac{1}{n+1}\to 0 \) factor AND inverts the ratio.
This assumes the ratio test limit for a factorial-free geometric series (\( L=1 \)), the boundary case.
This mistakes the numerator parameter for the ratio-test limit.
This mistakes the denominator parameter for the ratio-test limit.
This mistakenly applies the central-binomial factor of \( 4 \) here, but there is no binomial coefficient.

stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = n!\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{n} \). Apply the ratio test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| \).
solution: \( \dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n} = (n+1)\cdot\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} \), and as \( n\to\infty \) this grows without bound, so \( L = \infty \). Since \( L>1 \), the factorial in the numerator forces divergence regardless of \( p,q \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the growing factor \( (n+1) \) entirely, treating this as if it were a plain geometric series.
This drops \( (n+1) \) AND inverts the ratio.
This assumes the boundary ratio-test value for a factorial-free geometric series.
This wrongly assumes the factorial in the numerator forces the ratio to vanish, the opposite of what happens.
This mistakenly applies the central-binomial factor of \( 4 \) here, but there is no binomial coefficient.
This mistakes the numerator parameter for the ratio-test limit.

stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dfrac{n^{n}}{n!}\left(\dfrac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\right)^{n} \). Apply the ratio test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\dfrac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \( \left(\frac{n+1}{n}\right)^n \to e \) growth factor entirely.
This drops the \( e \) factor AND inverts the ratio.
This uses \( 1/e \) instead of \( e \), the reciprocal of the correct growth factor.
This assumes the boundary ratio-test value for a plain geometric series.
This mistakenly applies the central-binomial factor of \( 4 \) here instead of \( e \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
